items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
items.title: Identifying Alarm Features in Indigestion
items.content: Alarm features in indigestion suggest the need for further investigation to exclude serious underlying conditions. Key alarm features include unexplained weight loss, recurrent vomiting, dysphagia, occult or gross bleeding, nocturnal symptoms, jaundice, palpable mass or adenopathy, fever, and a family history of gastrointestinal neoplasm.
keyPoints: Alarm features necessitate further diagnostic evaluation.

Key alarm features: unexplained weight loss, recurrent vomiting, dysphagia, occult or gross bleeding, nocturnal symptoms, jaundice, palpable mass or adenopathy, fever, family history of gastrointestinal neoplasm.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
items.title: Empirical Treatment for Typical GERD
items.content: Patients with typical GERD symptoms and no alarm features can be treated empirically without further evaluation. The recommended treatment duration is 4-8 weeks using a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) acid suppressant, either as single or double dosing.
keyPoints: Empirical treatment for typical GERD: 4-8 weeks with PPI.
No further evaluation needed if no alarm features are present.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
items.title: Diagnostic Testing for Dyspepsia in Patients <60 Years Old
items.content: For patients under 60 years old with dyspepsia and no alarm features, initial diagnostic testing should assess H. pylori status using fecal antigen or urea breath testing. Those positive for H. pylori receive eradication therapy, while those negative are given empiric PPI therapy.
keyPoints: Initial test for dyspepsia in patients <60: H. pylori assessment via fecal antigen or urea breath test.
H. pylori positive patients receive eradication therapy; negative patients receive PPI therapy.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | DIAGNOSTIC TESTING

items.subtopic: LIFESTYLE, DIET, AND NONMEDICATION RECOMMENDATIONS | ACID-SUPPRESSING OR -NEUTRALIZING MEDICATIONS
items.title: Nonpharmacologic Management of GERD and Functional Dyspepsia
items.content: For patients with mild indigestion or functional dyspepsia, lifestyle modifications are recommended as initial management. This includes dietary adjustments such as reducing intake of fat, spicy foods, caffeine, alcohol, and specific exclusions like lactose for lactase deficiency and gluten for celiac disease. GERD patients should also limit ethanol, caffeine, chocolate, tobacco use, and adopt habits like smaller low-fat meals, avoiding snacks before bedtime, wearing loose clothing, and elevating the head of the bed.
keyPoints: Mild indigestion management includes reassurance and lifestyle changes without further intervention.
GERD patients should limit ethanol, caffeine, chocolate, tobacco; consume smaller low-fat meals; avoid late-night snacks; wear loose clothing; elevate bed head.
Functional dyspepsia dietary advice: reduce fat, spicy foods, caffeine, alcohol intake.
Lactose restriction for lactase deficiency and gluten exclusion for celiac disease are recommended.
Small studies suggest benefits of low FODMAP, six-food elimination, and gluten-free diets in functional dyspepsia.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · LIFESTYLE, DIET, AND NONMEDICATION RECOMMENDATIONS | ACID-SUPPRESSING OR -NEUTRALIZING MEDICATIONS

items.subtopic: ACID-SUPPRESSING OR -NEUTRALIZING MEDICATIONS
items.title: Pharmacologic Treatment Strategies for GERD and Functional Dyspepsia
items.content: For GERD, histamine H2 antagonists are useful in mild to moderate cases, while proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) are preferred for severe symptoms or erosive conditions. PPIs inhibit gastric H+, K+-ATPase and are more potent than H2 antagonists. In functional dyspepsia, especially the postprandial distress syndrome (PDS) subtype in H.pylori-negative patients, acid suppressants like PPIs are effective as initial therapy or for persistent symptoms after eradication. A meta-analysis showed a risk ratio of 0.88 favoring PPI over placebo.
keyPoints: Histamine H2 antagonists (e.g., cimetidine, famotidine) for mild to moderate GERD; PPIs (e.g., omeprazole, lansoprazole) for severe or erosive esophagitis.
PPIs inhibit gastric H+, K+-ATPase and are more potent than H2 antagonists.
Heartburn responds better to PPI therapy compared to regurgitation or atypical GERD symptoms.

Long-term PPI use may lead to complications like diarrhea, nutrient deficiencies (vitamin B12, iron, calcium), hypomagnesemia, bone demineralization, interstitial nephritis, and impaired medication absorption.
In functional dyspepsia, PPIs are recommended over H2 antagonists as first-line therapy; meta-analysis risk ratio of 0.88 favors PPI over placebo.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · LIFESTYLE, DIET, AND NONMEDICATION RECOMMENDATIONS | ACID-SUPPRESSING OR -NEUTRALIZING MEDICATIONS

items.subtopic: HELICOBACTER PYLORI ERADICATION
items.title: Eradication of H. pylori reduces dyspepsia symptoms with a modest risk reduction.
items.content: H. pylori eradication in functional dyspepsia results in a statistically significant 9% risk reduction compared to placebo. The pooled risk ratio from 25 controlled trials is 1.24 (95% CI: 1.12–1.37), indicating a benefit of eradication over placebo.
keyPoints: H. pylori eradication reduces dyspepsia symptoms with a 9% risk reduction compared to placebo.
Pooled risk ratio is 1.24 (95% CI: 1.12–1.37) in favor of H. pylori eradication over placebo.
Most drug combinations include 7-14 days of PPI plus two or three antibiotics, with or without bismuth products.
H. pylori infection is associated with reduced prevalence of GERD, and its eradication does not worsen GERD symptoms.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · HELICOBACTER PYLORI ERADICATION | AGENTS THAT MODIFY GASTROINTESTINAL MOTOR ACTIVITY | CENTRALLY ACTING NEUROMODULATORS

items.subtopic: AGENTS THAT MODIFY GASTROINTESTINAL MOTOR ACTIVITY
items.title: Baclofen reduces TLESRs by 40% in patients with refractory regurgitation.
items.content: The GABA-B agonist baclofen is effective in reducing esophageal exposure to refluxed gastric contents by decreasing transient lower esophageal sphincter relaxations (TLESRs) by 40%, making it useful for patients with refractory regurgitation of acid or nonacid fluid.
keyPoints: Baclofen, a GABA-B agonist, reduces TLESRs by 40%.
Effective in patients with refractory regurgitation of acid or nonacid fluid.
Prokinetic agents show 33% relative risk reductions in functional dyspepsia but have concerns about publication bias and small sample sizes.
PDS subtype may preferentially respond to prokinetic drugs.
Acotiamide, approved in Japan and India for functional dyspepsia, enhances gastric emptying and accommodation by increasing acetylcholine release.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · HELICOBACTER PYLORI ERADICATION | AGENTS THAT MODIFY GASTROINTESTINAL MOTOR ACTIVITY | CENTRALLY ACTING NEUROMODULATORS

items.subtopic: OTHER OPTIONS
items.title: Nonabsorbable Antibiotics and Probiotics in Functional Dyspepsia
items.content: Rifaximin, a nonabsorbable antibiotic, has shown potential benefits for functional dyspepsia. Similarly, the probiotic Lactobacillus gasseri may improve symptoms.
keyPoints: Nonabsorbable antibiotic rifaximin suggested to benefit functional dyspepsia in one trial.
Probiotic Lactobacillus gasseri reported to improve symptoms of functional dyspepsia.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · OTHER OPTIONS

items.subtopic: OTHER OPTIONS
items.title: Indications and Considerations for Antireflux Surgery in GERD
items.content: Fundoplication is an option for young GERD patients needing lifelong therapy, with typical heartburn responsive to PPIs and confirmed acid reflux on pH monitoring. However, poor response factors include atypical symptoms, absence of pathologic acid reflux, esophageal motor disturbances, and delayed gastric emptying.
keyPoints: Fundoplication for young GERD patients requiring lifelong therapy with typical heartburn responsive to PPIs.
Confirmed acid reflux on pH monitoring is a prerequisite for surgery.
~60% of patients develop recurrent GERD symptoms post-fundoplication.
Poor response factors: atypical symptoms, no pathologic acid reflux, esophageal motor disturbances, delayed gastric emptying.
items.reference: APPROACH TO THE PATIENT · OTHER OPTIONS

items.assertion: Upper endoscopy should be performed for patients with heartburn lasting more than 5 years.

items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Thyroid chemistries or calcium levels should be obtained for all patients with dyspepsia.
items.reason: These tests screen for metabolic etiologies of dyspepsia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these tests are only indicated in suspected cases of metabolic etiology.
